2	"""The orchestrator: a TTL key-value store.
3	
4	This file holds TWO versions of the store:
5	
6	  1. TTLStore           — the CLEAN, single-threaded core. Zero locks. The one
7	                          concurrency seam is a do-nothing `_lock()` context
8	                          manager that just yields. Read it top to bottom and
9	                          it reads like ordinary single-threaded code.
10	
11	  2. ThreadSafeTTLStore — a subclass that overrides ONLY `_lock()` to take a
12	                          real reentrant lock, and adds a background sweeper
13	                          thread. The core logic is inherited untouched.
14	
15	The interesting part of THIS problem is expiry runs on TWO clocks at once:
16	LAZY expiry on read (get() drops a key it finds expired) AND an ACTIVE sweeper
17	thread that periodically reaps expired keys so dead entries do not pile up
18	between reads. Both must agree, and neither may double-free or miscount.
19	"""
20	
21	import threading
22	from collections.abc import Iterator
23	from contextlib import contextmanager
24	
25	from gateways import Clock, SystemClock
26	from models import Entry, InvalidKeyError, InvalidTTLError
27	
28	
29	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
30	# TTLStore is the brain. It owns the one dict mapping key -> Entry and the
31	# injected clock. Every public call flows through here. The expiry RULE lives
32	# on the Entry; the store just enforces WHEN to apply it (on read, on count,
33	# and when the sweeper asks).
34	# 🎙️ "The store owns one map from key to Entry plus an injected clock. It never
35	#     reads the wall clock itself and it never recomputes expiry — it asks the
36	#     Entry. This clean version has no locks at all; the thread-safe version is
37	#     a 15-line subclass."
38	class TTLStore:
39	    """In-memory key-value store where every key has a time-to-live."""
40	
41	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
42	        # WHY: default to the real clock so production callers write
43	        #      TTLStore(); tests pass a FakeClock to control expiry.
44	        self._clock: Clock = clock or SystemClock()
45	        # WHY: a plain dict gives O(1) put/get/delete by key, which is exactly
46	        #      the operation set. A sweep is O(n) but runs off the hot path.
47	        self._entries: dict[str, Entry] = {}
48	
49	    # ── the concurrency seam (Template Method) ──────────────────────────────
50	    # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — it just yields. The whole
51	    #      file below is written as if single-threaded. The thread-safe
52	    #      subclass overrides this one method with a real RLock. That is the
53	    #      entire diff between "clean" and "thread-safe".
54	    @contextmanager
55	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
56	        yield
57	
58	    # ── public surface ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
59	    def put(self, key: str, value: object, ttl: float) -> None:
60	        """Store value under key, alive for ttl seconds from now."""
61	        self._validate_key(key)
62	        self._validate_ttl(ttl)
63	
64	        # WHY: snapshot the clock ONCE, before the lock, so the deadline is the
65	        #      same regardless of how long we wait for the lock. expires_at is
66	        #      absolute (now + ttl), computed here so the Entry stays dumb data.
67	        expires_at = self._clock.now() + ttl
68	
69	        with self._lock():
70	            # WHY: a put for an existing key REPLACES it — new value, fresh TTL.
71	            #      Assigning over the key is exactly that; no special-casing.
72	            self._entries[key] = Entry(value=value, expires_at=expires_at)
73	
74	    def get(self, key: str) -> object | None:
75	        """Return the live value for key, or None if missing or expired."""
76	        self._validate_key(key)
77	        now = self._clock.now()
78	
79	        with self._lock():
80	            entry = self._entries.get(key)
81	
82	            if entry is None:
83	                # WHY: a missing key returns None — get is a lookup, not an
84	                #      assertion. Callers that want "must exist" check for None.
85	                return None
86	
87	            if entry.is_expired(now):
88	                # WHY: LAZY expiry. We found a dead key on the read path, so we
89	                #      reclaim it right now instead of waiting for the sweeper.
90	                #      pop(key, None) is safe even if a racing sweeper already
91	                #      removed it — the second remove is a no-op, never an error.
92	                self._entries.pop(key, None)
93	                return None
94	
95	            return entry.value
96	
97	    def delete(self, key: str) -> bool:
98	        """Remove key. Returns True if it was present (and live), else False."""
99	        self._validate_key(key)
100	        now = self._clock.now()
101	
102	        with self._lock():
103	            entry = self._entries.get(key)
104	
105	            if entry is None or entry.is_expired(now):
106	                # WHY: deleting a missing OR already-expired key reports False —
107	                #      from the caller's view that key was not live, so there
108	                #      was nothing to delete. We still drop a found-expired key.
109	                self._entries.pop(key, None)
110	                return False
111	
112	            del self._entries[key]
113	            return True
114	
115	    def active_count(self) -> int:
116	        """Number of currently-live (non-expired) keys."""
117	        now = self._clock.now()
118	
119	        with self._lock():
120	            # WHY: count by asking each Entry if it is alive at `now`. We do NOT
121	            #      trust the raw len(dict) because expired-but-not-yet-swept
122	            #      keys may still sit in the map. This is the count the spec
123	            #      calls "active" — live keys only.
124	            return sum(1 for entry in self._entries.values() if not entry.is_expired(now))
125	
126	    def sweep_expired(self) -> int:
127	        """Reclaim every expired key right now. Returns how many were removed.
128	
129	        A background reaper thread calls this on a timer so dead entries do not
130	        accumulate between reads. It is also safe to call by hand (tests do).
131	        """
132	        now = self._clock.now()
133	
134	        with self._lock():
135	            # WHY: collect the dead keys FIRST, then delete — never mutate a
136	            #      dict while iterating it, that raises RuntimeError. One pass
137	            #      to find, one pass to remove.
138	            dead = [key for key, entry in self._entries.items() if entry.is_expired(now)]
139	
140	            for key in dead:
141	                # WHY: pop(key, None), not del. Lazy expiry on a reader thread
142	                #      may have removed this same key a microsecond ago. pop is
143	                #      the safe-to-call-twice form, so the sweeper and a reader
144	                #      can both decide a key is dead without one of them
145	                #      crashing on a double-free.
146	                self._entries.pop(key, None)
147	
148	            return len(dead)
149	
150	    # ── validation helpers (typed errors only) ──────────────────────────────
151	    def _validate_key(self, key: str) -> None:
152	        # WHY: reject bad keys at the boundary, before touching the map. An
153	        #      empty string or a non-string is a caller bug — fail loud and
154	        #      typed, do not silently store under "".
155	        if not isinstance(key, str) or key == "":
156	            raise InvalidKeyError(f"key must be a non-empty string, got {key!r}")
157	
158	    def _validate_ttl(self, ttl: float) -> None:
159	        # WHY: a ttl <= 0 means "already dead" — almost certainly a caller bug,
160	        #      so we reject it rather than storing a key that vanishes instantly.
161	        if not isinstance(ttl, (int, float)) or isinstance(ttl, bool) or ttl <= 0:
162	            raise InvalidTTLError(f"ttl must be a positive number of seconds, got {ttl!r}")
163	
164	
165	# ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
166	# ThreadSafeTTLStore is the §9 "second version". It changes exactly one thing
167	# about the logic: _lock() now takes a real reentrant lock. It also runs a
168	# background sweeper thread so expired keys get reaped even with no reads. All
169	# of put/get/delete/active_count/sweep_expired are inherited UNCHANGED.
170	# 🎙️ "The thread-safe version overrides one method — _lock now grabs a real
171	#     RLock. RLock not Lock because a public method holding the lock may call
172	#     another guarded helper on the same thread; a plain Lock would deadlock on
173	#     the re-entry. I also start a daemon sweeper thread that calls the
174	#     inherited sweep_expired on a timer."
175	class ThreadSafeTTLStore(TTLStore):
176	    """Thread-safe store with a background expiry sweeper."""
177	
178	    def __init__(self, clock: Clock | None = None, sweep_interval: float = 1.0) -> None:
179	        super().__init__(clock=clock)
180	        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) — the SAME thread can re-acquire it. If a future
181	        #      guarded method calls another guarded method, a plain Lock would
182	        #      deadlock; RLock lets the owning thread re-enter and only releases
183	        #      when the outermost `with` exits.
184	        self._mutex = threading.RLock()
185	        self._sweep_interval = sweep_interval
186	        self._stop = threading.Event()
187	        self._sweeper: threading.Thread | None = None
188	
189	    # WHY: the ONLY logic override. Same shape as the no-op above, but now it
190	    #      actually guards the dict. Every public method already wraps its
191	    #      critical section in `with self._lock()`, so this single override
192	    #      makes all of them thread-safe — zero changes to the core file.
193	    @contextmanager
194	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
195	        with self._mutex:
196	            yield
197	
198	    def start_sweeper(self) -> None:
199	        """Launch the background reaper thread (idempotent)."""
200	        if self._sweeper is not None and self._sweeper.is_alive():
201	            # WHY: safe to call twice — second call does nothing. We do not want
202	            #      two sweeper threads racing the same map.
203	            return
204	
205	        self._stop.clear()
206	        self._sweeper = threading.Thread(target=self._sweep_loop, daemon=True)
207	        # WHY: daemon=True so the reaper never blocks process shutdown.
208	        self._sweeper.start()
209	
210	    def stop_sweeper(self) -> None:
211	        """Signal the sweeper to exit and wait for it (idempotent)."""
212	        self._stop.set()
213	
214	        thread = self._sweeper
215	
216	        if thread is not None:
217	            # WHY: join with a timeout so a wedged sweeper can never hang the
218	            #      caller forever. wait() inside the loop returns promptly once
219	            #      _stop is set, so this normally returns immediately.
220	            thread.join(timeout=self._sweep_interval + 1.0)
221	            self._sweeper = None
222	
223	    def _sweep_loop(self) -> None:
224	        # WHY: Event.wait(timeout) is the sleep — but it wakes instantly when
225	        #      stop_sweeper sets the event, so shutdown is not delayed by a full
226	        #      interval. The loop exits as soon as _stop is set.
227	        while not self._stop.wait(self._sweep_interval):
228	            # WHY: reuse the inherited sweep_expired UNCHANGED. It already takes
229	            #      _lock() (now the real RLock), so the reap is atomic against
230	            #      put/get/delete. The sweeper adds no new logic, just a clock.
231	            self.sweep_expired()
232

1	# Python 3.11+
2	"""Narrated demo driver for the TTL key-value store.
3	
4	Run me with:  python main.py
5	Each case prints "Case N ... -> OK" so a green run is obvious at a glance.
6	The FakeClock lets us "fast-forward time" without ever sleeping.
7	"""
8	
9	from gateways import FakeClock
10	from models import InvalidKeyError, InvalidTTLError
11	from ttl_store_service import ThreadSafeTTLStore, TTLStore
12	
13	
14	def case_1_put_get_alive() -> None:
15	    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
16	    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)
17	
18	    store.put("session", "alice", ttl=10)
19	    # WHY: at t=3 the key is well within its 10s TTL, so get returns the value.
20	    clock.advance(3)
21	    assert store.get("session") == "alice", "live key must return its value"
22	    assert store.active_count() == 1, "one live key expected"
23	
24	    print("Case 1 put then get within TTL returns the value -> OK")
25	
26	
27	def case_2_lazy_expiry_on_read() -> None:
28	    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
29	    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)
30	
31	    store.put("otp", "123456", ttl=5)
32	    # WHY: jump to exactly the deadline. is_expired uses >=, so the key dies AT
33	    #      t=5, not after — this proves the boundary, the classic off-by-one.
34	    clock.advance(5)
35	    assert store.get("otp") is None, "expired key must read as None"
36	    assert store.active_count() == 0, "expired key must not count as active"
37	
38	    print("Case 2 key read at its exact deadline is expired and reclaimed -> OK")
39	
40	
41	def case_3_overwrite_resets_ttl() -> None:
42	    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
43	    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)
44	
45	    store.put("k", "v1", ttl=10)
46	    clock.advance(8)
47	    # WHY: re-putting the same key replaces the value AND resets the TTL clock.
48	    #      So at t=8 we overwrite; the new key now lives until t=8+10=18.
49	    store.put("k", "v2", ttl=10)
50	    clock.advance(5)  # now t=13 — old TTL (t=10) passed, new TTL (t=18) not yet
51	    assert store.get("k") == "v2", "overwrite must reset the TTL and value"
52	
53	    print("Case 3 re-putting a key resets its value and TTL -> OK")
54	
55	
56	def case_4_delete_and_invalid_inputs() -> None:
57	    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
58	    store = TTLStore(clock=clock)
59	
60	    store.put("a", 1, ttl=10)
61	    assert store.delete("a") is True, "deleting a live key reports True"
62	    assert store.delete("a") is False, "deleting a missing key reports False"
63	    assert store.get("a") is None, "deleted key is gone"
64	
65	    # WHY: every public boundary rejects bad input with a TYPED error, before
66	    #      mutating any state.
67	    for bad_key in ("", 123):
68	        try:
69	            store.put(bad_key, "x", ttl=5)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
70	            assert False, "bad key should have raised"
71	        except InvalidKeyError:
72	            pass
73	
74	    for bad_ttl in (0, -3, "5"):
75	        try:
76	            store.put("ok", "x", ttl=bad_ttl)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
77	            assert False, "bad ttl should have raised"
78	        except InvalidTTLError:
79	            pass
80	
81	    print("Case 4 delete is exact and bad key or ttl raises a typed error -> OK")
82	
83	
84	def case_5_active_sweeper_reaps_dead_keys() -> None:
85	    clock = FakeClock(start=0.0)
86	    store = ThreadSafeTTLStore(clock=clock, sweep_interval=0.02)
87	
88	    store.put("short", "x", ttl=5)
89	    store.put("long", "y", ttl=100)
90	
91	    # WHY: move the clock past the short key's deadline, then let the background
92	    #      sweeper thread wake and reap. We did NOT call get() on "short", so the
93	    #      ONLY way it leaves the map is the active sweeper — that is the point.
94	    clock.advance(10)
95	    store.start_sweeper()
96	    try:
97	        _wait_until(lambda: store.active_count() == 1, timeout=2.0)
98	    finally:
99	        store.stop_sweeper()
100	
101	    assert store.get("short") is None, "sweeper must reap the expired key"
102	    assert store.get("long") == "y", "live key must survive the sweep"
103	    assert store.active_count() == 1, "exactly one live key remains"
104	
105	    print("Case 5 background sweeper reaps expired keys, live keys survive -> OK")
106	
107	
108	def _wait_until(predicate, timeout: float) -> None:
109	    import time
110	
111	    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout
112	
113	    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
114	        if predicate():
115	            return
116	        time.sleep(0.005)
117	
118	    assert predicate(), "condition not met within timeout"
119	
120	
121	def main() -> None:
122	    case_1_put_get_alive()
123	    case_2_lazy_expiry_on_read()
124	    case_3_overwrite_resets_ttl()
125	    case_4_delete_and_invalid_inputs()
126	    case_5_active_sweeper_reaps_dead_keys()
127	    print("\nAll cases passed.")
128	
129	
130	if __name__ == "__main__":
131	    main()
